_pass: Level 3 Pass 1 + Rounds 1-3 visual polish + worked examples v2
title: Rounding and estimation
deck: Rounding swaps a messy number for a nice one nearby. Estimation chains rounded numbers together to predict an answer in seconds, and to catch errors a calculator would never see.
objectives: Use rounding and estimation to solve application problems.

content.text: Imagine sitting down at a small restaurant with a friend. We each order a burger, and we share a basket of fries and two drinks. When the check arrives, the total reads $487. Without any pencil work, we already know something is off. Two burgers, two drinks, and an order of fries should land near fifty dollars, not five hundred.

That instinct, the one that flagged the bill before a calculator ever appeared, is estimation. It quietly protects us every time we shop, every time we cook, and every time we hand over a card. In this section we make the move deliberate: round each piece of a problem to a nicer number, chain the pieces together, and use the result as a sanity check against any exact computation we trust less than our own head.
1.3.1  Rounding finds the nearest nice number

To round a number means to replace it with a simpler number nearby, usually one that ends in zeros or has fewer decimal places. The replacement is chosen to keep the original number close, while making the arithmetic that follows much easier to do in our heads.

The rule is short. Choose the place value we want to round to. Look at the digit immediately to its right. If that digit is 5 or more, round the chosen digit up; if it is less than 5, leave it alone. Drop everything to the right of the chosen place. That is the entire mechanism, applied to whole numbers and decimals alike.

content.caption: Figure 1.3. Rounding is really just "which nice number is this closer to?" Past the midpoint it rounds up; before the midpoint it rounds down.

content.text: Definition 1.3. To round a number to a given place value is to replace it with the nearest number that ends in zeros (or stops) at that place. To estimate a quantity is to compute an approximate value by replacing each piece of the calculation with a rounded number, then performing the arithmetic on the simpler numbers.

Margin noteWhen the digit to the right is exactly 5 and the rest of the number is zero, conventions split. Most introductory texts (and this one) round up; some scientific contexts round to the nearest even digit, called “banker's rounding,” to reduce long-run bias.
1.3.2  Rounding decimals follows the same rule

Decimals behave identically. To round 7.4839 to the nearest hundredth, we look at the hundredths digit (8), peek one place to the right (3), see that 3 is less than 5, and so we leave the 8 alone and drop everything after it. The result is 7.48. The same procedure rounds 6.7825 to the nearest tenth: the tenths digit is 7, the next digit is 8, so the 7 climbs to an 8, giving 6.8.

Common mistakeTruncating instead of rounding. Truncating means simply chopping off the digits to the right; rounding means looking at the very next digit first. Truncating 4,587 to the nearest hundred gives 4,500, which is wrong. Rounding it gives 4,600.
1.3.3  Estimation chains rounded numbers

Rounding simplifies one number. Estimation simplifies a whole calculation by rounding each piece and then doing the easy arithmetic on the simpler pieces. The trick is that small individual errors tend to cancel out when we add: half the items round up by a little, half round down by a little, and the totals stay close to the true value.

Example 1.3. Predicting the grocery total in our head.

We are standing in the checkout line with eight items in the cart, prices ranging from a few dollars to about eight. We want to know roughly what the receipt will say, for budget, for sanity, and to catch a scanning error if there is one.

Solution.

Step 1. Choose a place value. For dollars and cents the easiest target is the nearest dollar, which drops the cents entirely and leaves whole numbers we can add in our heads.

Step 2. Round each item. Look at the cents on each price. If we see 50 cents or more, round up; otherwise round down. Suppose the eight rounded prices come out to

3, 8, 2, 6, 1, 8, 5, 7

Step 3. Add in convenient groups. Pair the numbers however feels easiest:

(3 + 8) + (2 + 6) + (1 + 8) + (5 + 7) = 11 + 8 + 9 + 12 = 40

Step 4. Compare to reality. The receipt totals $41.12. Our $40 estimate is off by about a dollar, on a $40 bill, after roughly ten seconds of mental math.

If the receipt had instead said $89, we would know to look closer; if it had said $400, we would know without thinking that something was wrong. That is the real reason to estimate: not to replace the calculator, but to keep it honest.

In the wildProject budgets, contractor quotes, and dinner-bill splits all begin with the same move: round each line item to something tidy, add the rounded numbers, and use the result as a yardstick before any exact figure is computed. Bookkeepers call it a “ballpark reconciliation,” and it catches typos that would otherwise slip into a spreadsheet unnoticed.
1.3.4  Estimation as an error check

Calculators are exact, but they are also obedient: they faithfully compute whatever we typed in, including the wrong thing. If we accidentally press an extra zero, the calculator returns a confidently wrong answer. A quick estimate is the independent check that keeps us honest. When our head says “about 50” and the screen says “4,892,” we know to look again at our input.
A calculator computes; estimation supervises.
Exercises

1. Round 4,587 to the nearest hundred.

    Show answer

    Answer. 4,600. The hundreds digit is 5; the digit to its right is 8, which is at least 5, so the 5 hundreds becomes 6 hundreds.

  2. Round 7.4839 to the nearest hundredth.

    Show answer

    Answer. 7.48. The hundredths digit is 8; the next digit (thousandths) is 3, which is less than 5, so the 8 stays and everything after is dropped.

  3. A receipt arrives with a total of $87. The cart contained three cereals at $4.99 each, two milks at $3.79 each, and one bread at $3.49. Use estimation to decide whether $87 is a reasonable total.

    Show answer

    Answer. No. Rounded: 3 × $5 + 2 × $4 + 1 × $3 ≈ $26. The actual sum is $26.04, so the receipt is more than three times too high. Something has been scanned multiple times or an unwanted fee has been added.

  4. Round 8,672 to the nearest thousand.

    1. 8,000
    2. 8,600
    3. 8,700
    4. 9,000
    Show answer

    Answer. D (9,000). The thousands digit is 8; the next digit is 6, which is at least 5, so the 8 thousands becomes 9 thousands. Option A is truncation, not rounding.

  5. Why is estimation useful even when a calculator is in our pocket?

    1. It catches input errors and unreasonable answers.
    2. Calculators are usually wrong.
    3. It is faster than typing into a calculator.
    4. It is more impressive at a job interview.
    Show answer

    Answer. A. The calculator computes whatever was typed; estimation supervises whether the result is in the right ballpark.

  6. Round 6.7825 to the nearest tenth.

    1. 6.7
    2. 6.78
    3. 6.8
    4. 7
    Show answer

    Answer. C (6.8). The tenths digit is 7; the next digit is 8, so the 7 rounds up to 8.
More worked examples

content.title: Rounding to a specified place
content.solution:
**Find the rounding place.** The hundredths digit is the second after the decimal point — here, **8** (the second 8 in 3.78294 is the hundredth).

Wait — careful. The decimal is 3.**7**8294. Position 1 after the point is 7 (tenths). Position 2 is 8 (hundredths). Position 3 is 2 (thousandths).

**Look at one place further right.** The thousandths digit is **2**. Since 2 is less than 5, we round *down*: the hundredths digit stays at 8.

Answer: **3.78**.

content.title: The 'look one place right' rule, not the whole tail
content.solution:
**Find the rounding place.** The ones place — here, **7**.

**Look one place to the right.** The tenths digit is **4**. Since 4 is less than 5, we round *down*: the ones digit stays at 7.

Answer: **47**.

*The trap to notice.* Students sometimes look at the *whole tail* (here, .499) and reason "that's almost 0.5, so we should round up." The convention is to look at **only one** digit past the rounding place — and 4 is less than 5, so the answer is 47 even though 47.5 (close by) would round up to 48.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Rounding has a clean recipe: identify the rounding place, look exactly **one** digit past it, and round up if that digit is 5 or more, down if it is 4 or less. The rest of the trailing digits do not matter to the rule. Estimation is rounding done before calculation, to check whether an answer is in the right ballpark.

content.text: Looking ahead

Phrases such as “$50 a week” or “60 miles per hour” are also approximate statements, but they describe how much something changes per unit: dollars per week, miles per hour. That kind of quantity has its own name in mathematics. Section 1.4 introduces it as slope, and turns the loose approximations of estimation into precise rates of change.
1.3
